data.title: Tariffs Cancelled: Risk Eliminated Or A Death Trap?
data.description:
The funny side

The verdict was hard, but truthfully, many had already accepted it as inevitable, and anyone who says anything else is either lying or doesn't know how the system works.

Now, turning to the facts, the U.S. Supreme Court, by a strong majority, declared unconstitutional, under the provisions of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act of 1977, the authority to place tariffs on a global basis, thus dismantling one of the current administration’s most aggressive international trade policy instruments.

Wall Street reacted very quickly to the news. Once the news hit the terminals and spread across financial media, the normal buy-the-rally began. At this time, the Nasdaq (NDX) is up approximately 0.53% while the S&P 500 (SPX) is up 0.30%. The markets seem to have taken the news fairly well, at least for now. Markets perceived the court decision as a reduction in trade tensions and therefore bought risk with little hesitation.

However, I wouldn't make it that simple. In my last article, I wrote that accepting that the administration would be defeated could be dangerous considering the administration will absorb the damage but will reorganize and will most likely pursue other legal avenues, which will be less visible and less noisy than the tweets we have grown accustomed to in 2025. These will be just as effective as anything else the administration pursues.

Personally, I think this euphoria we are seeing now could be only a short-term sigh of relief and not the beginning of a long-term structural trend.

So fasten your seat belts and watch your step.

Underestimated risk

In my view, today we are taking the positive news while either ignoring the negative or refusing to acknowledge it. Clearly, equities have taken the good news and priced in the possibility of higher corporate profit margins, yet Treasuries seem less confident with the potential for increasing yields as I write this. In my view, equities are underestimating the second- and third-order implications of the rulings from a fiscal perspective. If the removal of tariffs results in large refund checks to importers (we're not discussing crumbs here, but rather over $170 billion), then the U.S. Treasury Department may face a large and immediate increase in its funding requirements, particularly in the very short term (T-Bill).

As previously stated, tariff risk has not diminished; it has simply altered its form. It is possible that the Administration may attempt to implement additional statutory means (i.e., Sections 122 & 338) to continue the high level of uncertainty for both politics and commerce.

Mispricing

The U.S. Supreme Court held (in a 6-3 decision) that tariffs may only be implemented by Congressional action pursuant to Article I, Section 8 of the U.S. Constitution, which is to say the administration's position under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) was rejected. Put simply, the Court concluded that tariffs cannot be treated as "regulatory" and therefore subject to presidential authority.

So far, so good for the market and, specifically, for equities. But, in my opinion, the market is moving too quickly down the logical path of how this decision will ultimately affect the economy. The equities markets continue to look almost exclusively at corporate income statements and pay little attention to the federal government's balance sheet at this critical juncture.

In my opinion, this case also does not mark the end of the controversy surrounding tariffs but rather marks the beginning of a potentially more contentious phase of the controversy, both legally and politically.

The next arena of uncertainty is the matter of refunds. The Supreme Court did not consider the issue of whether those who have paid tariffs for goods imported into the U.S. during the period in which tariffs were illegal will be entitled to a refund. This question has been left to the lower courts to resolve.

There are currently over 1,500 cases filed in the U.S. Court of International Trade by importers, including Costco and Goodyear, seeking refunds for tariffs paid on their imports. While if only a few percent of these petitions result in actual refunds, the amount of money refunded back to the importers (and therefore deducted from federal coffers) could be substantial.

Therefore, while the equities markets can now avoid a potential future tax increase, the fixed-income markets (bonds) will need to factor in a bill that has already been incurred.

The Plan "B"

To say that “the era of tariffs is over” is rather simplistic, as I mentioned earlier. As I also mentioned previously, the Administration has a Plan B. It’s likely to be true.

There are additional legal instruments at the disposal of the Executive Branch. These will not provide exactly the same level of effect as the IEEPA, but they can provide similar effects; the difference will be that the path will be more circuitous (and slower), which could create more instability in the marketplace.

The political decision is nearly an inevitability, since, in my view, if the Administration were to give up entirely on the tariff leverage, it would be perceived as weaker, and the run-up to the midterm elections would not be the right time to appear weaker! If it reuses the tariff, then it will bring uncertainty back into focus. Volatility exists in either case.

As shown on the attached table, IEEPA has been blocked. However, there are many alternative tools.

For example, the two most commonly used tools (Section 232, national security, and Section 201, serious injury or threat of serious injury to a domestic industry) need the involvement of a federal agency (U.S. Department of Commerce & U.S. International Trade Commission, respectively). Therefore, these tools will involve lengthy delays, as well as numerous technical requirements and public dossiers. Thus, they cannot be quickly activated.

Similarly, Section 301, which involves a USTR investigation into discriminatory practices, can take years and does not have a maximum duration. Therefore, it is a powerful tool, but slow.

On the other hand, Sections 122 and 338 do not require a preliminary government investigation. This is what makes them different. Section 122 provides for intervention in cases where "international payments problems" arise; tariffs can be levied up to 15% for 150 days. The 150-day time frame is limited. Nevertheless, this time frame can be extended by Congress. More importantly, however, Section 122 is a rapid action tool. Given the hypersensitivity of current markets, 150 days is an eternity.

Even more complex is Section 338, which authorizes tariffs of up to 50% in the event of trade discrimination against the U.S. Like Section 122, Section 338 does not have a specified time frame. Moreover, like Section 122, Section 338 is a rarely used provision in modern times. Therefore, there is enormous interpretive uncertainty associated with Section 338.

Thus, do not celebrate too much today. Tariffs could come back, simply through another door. The tools requiring investigations will provide months of waiting and speculation. The tools that do not require investigations can produce immediate shock effects. Regardless of whether it takes months or immediate action, the result is not a stable environment; rather, it is an environment of structural uncertainty.

Intellectual Honesty

There is always a possibility for markets to continue to be misaligned with my thesis; therefore, to be frank, although I think the present situation is delicate, I don't consider it to be without alternatives. There are many risks involved in this scenario, as you cannot simply be incorrect regarding the numbers; you also must be correct with regard to the timing.

There are at least three possible scenarios that could render my thesis invalid:

#1 The "Quagmire" of Refunds

It is possible refunds are not immediate, or even worse, that they will be made in some type of installment plan over years of litigation. Litigation may take a long time. With sovereign immunity, appeal options, administrative procedures, and lower court decisions, there is no guarantee that the U.S. Treasury will ever have to pay over $170 billion anytime soon. If the payments were made over an extended period of time (possibly ten years), then the supply shock in the T-bill market would be greatly diminished.

#2 Political Capitulation

The Administration has the option of not using tariffs again. I do not believe this is likely, but we will include it in our discussion. Section 232 and Section 301 both have significant and complicated investigation requirements. If the timeline was incompatible with the political calendar and Section 122 was not invoked, then the above picture would dramatically change. Then there would really be less uncertainty in trade. Retailer margins and discretionary consumer good margins could stabilize or grow. The current rally would no longer just be a technical rebound but could begin to take on some structural characteristics. I do not see this as my base case; however, it would be an error to not consider it.

#3 Geopolitical Shocks and Market Distortions

If the political climate in the Middle East worsens, a 'flight to quality' is probable; regardless of the deficits or yields on treasury bonds, investors will continue to purchase them at whatever cost. So even though the country's financial condition will continue to decline, the yield on Treasury bonds will go down.

Conclusion

This was one of the most historic rulings by the U.S. Supreme Court. It took away from the Executive Branch what has been deemed to be an unconstitutional tool (IEEPA). However, it did not resolve the trade dispute—it merely changed how the dispute will unfold.

When the U.S. stock market reacts positively as if we are entering into a period of normalcy with no trade disputes, I think this is too optimistic. Although the IEEPA is now gone, this does not mean all tariffs are gone; it simply means if tariffs return, they will do so through different avenues than before.

There are also repayment issues. Even if a portion of the estimated $170 billion owed under the previous Tariff Act is paid back relatively quickly, the U.S. Treasury Department will need to fund its operations. This means additional short-term funding needs, which is not good for long-term interest rates given the existing large deficit. It is not just about money flowing out of the country. When companies believe the trade environment could change on a quarterly basis, capital expenditures (CAPEX) tend to slow down. This is not due to a lack of funds available; rather, it is a lack of clarity as to what the future holds. When uncertainty increases, Boards of Directors are typically inclined to delay their decisions until clarity returns.

In my experience, intermittent risk is what the market fears the most. There is still some upside to the current rally in discretionary consumer products in the near term; however, without a structural reduction in uncertainty, I view the rally as very fragile.

The first round is over, and the real game has just begun.

data.title: CRH plc: Focus On International And Inorganic Growth
data.description:
I've continued to rate CRH plc (CRH) as 'Buy'.

The company's 4Q25 EBITDA beat isn't a one-off. I'm predicting a similar outperformance this year, considering its inorganic and international prospects.

Its aspirational medium-term targets and appealing valuations were detailed in my October 13, 2025, write-up.

Q4 Operating Profitability Bested Consensus

The firm issued an 8-K filing that revealed its latest financials on Wednesday, Feb 18.

CRH's EBITDA rose 14.2% year-on-year to $2.03B for Oct-Dec '25. As per Bloomberg, this came in +2% better than what the sell-side projected.

I believe the "International Solutions Segment/ISS" was the difference-maker.

ISS saw its 4Q25 EBITDA-to-sales expand 380bps to 19%. That drove up the division's EBITDA by 32.6% YoY to $0.63B over the same period.

This unit's solid results were attributed to pricing-related actions and corporate reorganization initiatives based on the 8-K disclosures.

Changes In Pricing/Volumes For The Recent Three-Month Period

As outlined above, CRH's construction product prices in foreign markets remained firm or trended higher during 4Q25.

It's reasonable for me to infer that ISS has a strong competitive position. 2025 was the "ninth consecutive year of good pricing across the European cement business" according to the analyst briefing.

Also, asset disposals contributed $19M to ISS's Q4 EBITDA increase.

CRH emphasized the "good portfolio optimization work undertaken during the year in the international division" at its earnings call. My view is that ISS should have exited non-strategic and less profitable businesses to enhance margins.

The company's group-level EBITDA-to-revenue improved +1.5ppts to 4Q2025's 21.5%. That surpassed the consensus's estimate by 40 bps. You would think that its overseas operations should take the credit.

I'm Anticipating A FY26 EBITDA Beat

In my opinion, CRH's full-year guide is excessively cautious.

It's forecasting an "Adjusted EBITDA of $8.1bn to $8.5bn" in the new fiscal year (source: 8-K). The $8,300M mid-point figure implies a growth deceleration from FY2025's +10.8% to FY2026's +8.1%.

At its 4Q25 results meeting, CRH acknowledged that the '26 outlook only incorporates the financial impact of "recently closed acquisitions" or "$200 million of net incremental EBITDA."

My prior mid-Oct '25 piece highlighted the firm's expectations that "its future sales increase is probably going to be split 50-50 between organic and inorganic." But the group's assumption of a $0.2B M&A-driven accretion is just one-third of this year's projected EBITDA increase.

CRH's "Free Cash Flow/FCF" jumped 17.6% to $4,969M in FY25 on YoY terms. It spent around $0.4B on a mid-teens number of deals in 4Q2025. My calculations imply that the FCF generated last year is sufficient to fund another 12 quarters of inorganic activity.

At its analyst call, management described the company as "the acquirer of choice" due to its "local relationships." I would agree with this statement. About four-fifths of its historical M&As are sourced by "teams (situated) across thousands of (domestic) locations" as per its Investor Day remarks.

I mentioned in the Oct 13, 2025 update that "there is room for further sector consolidation in CRH's major business areas."

One good example is water infrastructure. RBC published a report (not publicly available) titled "The $10bn Shape Of Water" on January 27, 2026. Its research suggests that "75% of US water players have less than 1% market share." RBC's analysis also draws attention to the 30+% EBITDA margin for CRH's water-focused businesses.

You can imagine that the enterprise's FY26 top-line and profitability will be positive surprises if it can add a few water-related companies to its portfolio.

Separately, ISS's favorable momentum will most probably be sustained.

CRH's 4Q briefing comments indicated that it's expecting a "good year in terms of pricing across the international business." Selected non-U.S. markets are experiencing a turnaround based on the management's observations.

My bet is on CRH delivering at least a $8,500M EBITDA (high-end of guidance) for FY2026. This implies a +10.7% expansion, on par with that in the previous year.

You Need To Contemplate These Bear-Case Scenarios

It's a possibility that the company executes on fewer-than-anticipated acquisitions. High-quality targets may not be available for sale. There could also be disagreement over the purchase consideration.

A weak global economy can lead to client push-backs on ISS's price hikes. This segment might not be able to replicate its strong 4Q2025 showing in the future.

The Final Word

I'm still a bull on this name.

I choose to benchmark CRH's valuations against listed comparables Vulcan Materials Company (VMC) and Martin Marietta Materials, Inc. (MLM). That's the same approach adopted in my previous article.

The forward FY26 EV/EBITDAs for CRH and VMC/MLM (average) are 12.1x and 17.9x (source: S&P Capital IQ), respectively.

The stock's re-rating upside is +48% (17.9/12.1). I see an above-expectations FY26 EBITDA as a likely catalyst.

data.title: Nu Holdings Q4 Preview: Nu Is Coming For The U.S. Peers
data.description:
Introduction

Every time I revisit a stock after I’ve already written an article about it, I am very excited to compare what has changed, not only with the business of the company but also with how I view and interpret their numbers and results. So that my judgment is not clouded by previous thoughts or opinions of other investors and analysts and my previous thoughts, I first like to open the latest results, come up with a thesis, and only after opening and comparing what I said about the stock in the past. This is exactly how I worked today with Nu Holdings Ltd. (NU), and the result is fascinating! It turns out not only did all the metrics that made me bullish about NU stay resilient, but the company has actually managed to accelerate in growth.

I am a shareholder of the stock myself, and I have actually entertained the idea of trimming my position after the rally you see on the graph below. The stock is up by more than 30%, while the S&P 500 (SP500) has gained only about 11% in the same timeframe.

The best part of this growth is the fact that NU isn’t a U.S.-based company, and this was one of the reasons I found this stock appealing. Not only did the business impress me, but its full exposure to Latin America gave me the diversification from the U.S. I wanted to add to my portfolio. At the same time, it’s fairly rare for a non-U.S.-based company to perform as well as the U.S. companies, simply because investor sentiment gets stronger for the latter, but in this case, Nu has managed to surprise me once again.

Since my coverage in June, many developments have occurred, and the company reports its earnings results on February 25th. Today, I’ll take a look at those developments, talk about the most important metrics, and discuss what to look out for on earnings.

Q3 2025

Writing my previous piece, I was really impressed by the 99 million active customers, with an activity rate of 83%. If you stop and think about that for a moment, it becomes hard to grasp the scale of this operation. SoFi Technologies (SOFI), a U.S. peer of Nu, has 13.5 million active customers, and it is a great result. This stock is not only popular among retail investors, but the huge institutions like JPMorgan Chase (JPM) also have substantial positions there.

But what then to think of NU? Not only is the user base so much larger, but it also increases every quarter. In the last five years, its compound annual growth rate (CAGR) is at 27%. Together with that, the quality of the users doesn’t erode with user growth, as the activity ratio holds up at 83%-84%, and the average revenue per active customer only goes up. It means the company doesn’t trade quantity for quality, and it could point to the real market advantage, which translates into a solid organic demand.

This growth rate would be impossible if the company stayed in one market - Brazil. It started that way, as it is a Brazilian company, but then, after capturing a substantial part of the Brazilian market (61% of the adult population as of today), it continued to the neighboring countries, which was a great decision. In all the Latin American countries, the pain of the consumer has been the same: large, expensive, bureaucratic institutions that were focused on revenues more than consumer interest. This is why Nu was so successful in Brazil at first and later in Colombia and Mexico. Besides, the mentality of the consumer is not entirely different, so it’s easier and faster for Nu to enter a new market after being an already trusted and established player in Brazil. The graph below shows the growth in Mexico, compared to that in Brazil, over the years.

Even though it’s way easier for Nu to grow in Mexico, as it is still a relatively young company there, and it shows by an enormous 105% CAGR there since 2019, Brazil's growth continues. At the same time, the company has switched its focus from expanding in users to selling more to those customers already acquired. Another brilliant decision, in my opinion, as there is almost no cost for the revenue increase that comes from old customers. This way, the company becomes more efficient, boosting not only the top line but also the margins and saving on other costs, like marketing, which impact the bottom line.

Lending growth is accelerating, powered by the mix of different segments. The largest part is, of course, credit card debt, although the ratio of it is slowly going down as the company adds to the mix secured and unsecured personal loans. In the U.S., SoFi had some major difficulties in the past for its overexposure to personal and student loans, but the situation with Nu is entirely different.

The company is known for being careful with robust credit loss allowances and strong coverage ratios. Its risk-adjusted net interest margin is at about 10%, even after accounting for credit loss provisions. This is an incredibly high level, although low enough to keep it sustainable. If something happens with the interest rates, this company will have to be able to work around that, but I believe the management is aware of that. Countries in Latin America can have more frequent changes in interest rates than more established countries, and it is a part of the market landscape Nu is used to. Its cash position is enormous in case it needs to cover some of the losses, but I hope it would never come to that.

Q4 2025

Going into the next quarter, this company is expected to continue growing rapidly. Revenue Estimate is now sitting at about $4.5 billion, which is a growth of about 50% from $3 billion one year ago.

Analysts' revisions are mostly positive, and it is not surprising, given the weakness of the U.S. dollar (DXY). It will be much easier for the company to impress the market by reporting in the U.S. dollar, which right now has reached an all-time low level compared to many currencies. The Brazilian real compared to the dollar is not far from being at the strongest level in the last five years.

At the end of January, we’ve got some major news, which hopefully will be addressed during the call. The company has received approval to begin operations as a bank in the United States. I have to be honest, this piece of news worries me slightly, as the U.S. market is entirely different from the markets Nu is used to. Its main advantage, its consumer focus, is not an advantage in the U.S., where the competition pushes all the banking organizations to be more efficient, cheaper, creative, and innovative. According to the news, the company is supposed to open this bank in the next 18 months:

With the approval, Nu ((NU)) is now in the bank organization phase, in which Nu satisfies certain OCC conditions alongside pending required approvals from the Federal Deposit Insurance Corp. and the Federal Reserve. During this phase, the company will focus on fully capitalizing the institution within 12 months and opening the bank within 18 months.

I would really like for the analysts on the call to ask about how the management views its competitive capabilities, as for now, the management has not given out specifics:

This approval isn't just an expansion of our operation; it’s an opportunity to prove our thesis that a digital-first, customer-centric model is the future of financial services globally... While we remain fully focused on our core markets in Brazil, Mexico, and Colombia, this step allows us to build the next generation of banking in the United States.

The U.S. competitive landscape is brutal, with not only huge players but also many regional and online banks. You can already find basically anything you are looking for - an account with no physical presence, an account in a few minutes, credit card issuance, online support, great pricing, no interest for new members, loan refinancing, etc. It is really hard to impress the U.S. consumer, and I doubt the company can casually enter the market while mostly focusing on Brazil, Mexico, and Colombia.

Wondering further on how Nu would want to treat that, I am curious about this large cash position (over $14 billion) that the company increases quarter over quarter. I could assume Nu would want to speed up the establishment of its presence in the U.S. by acquiring a bank that is already operating there. It’s, of course, a speculation on my part, but that would probably be the least attractive scenario for me. If the company has a way of doing things that worked out perfectly in one market, it doesn’t start from scratch in an entirely new market; the risk of misunderstanding the consumer is too large.

Besides this, I will, as always, watch out for the average revenue per customer growth in Brazil, which, in my opinion, is a far more important metric as of today. I expect the growth in Colombia and Mexico to accelerate and for Nu to continue capturing the market share there. Loan and credit health is important, although I trust the management to keep it tight. This is not the company that takes unnecessary risks for a quick profit boost, and I count on this behavior to continue.

Valuation

The stock is not cheap, especially if we take into account that it is not even a U.S.-based company. In my opinion, it deserves a premium for its brilliant execution, but the high valuation multiple might not allow the stock to rally as it did in 2025.

At the same time, it’s still way cheaper than SoFi. Nu trades at a TTM P/E of 33x, while SoFi is at over 50x. In my opinion, the PEG ratio is a far better way to evaluate high-growth stocks, and according to it, the stock is only 30% overvalued relative to the sector. But as I already said, this company deserves a premium, as its growth is organic and well-managed. The company is not losing quality just to present the results, and this is what matters the most.

Maybe the management hopes that with the established U.S. operation, the stock will get re-rated as a U.S.-based company?

There are some risks worth mentioning. The first risk is the political environment in the countries in which it operates. This year, Colombia and Brazil have elections, which potentially could provide both head and tailwinds depending on who wins. Mexico is going to have elections in 2027. Another risk is, of course, an execution. As the company grows and scales, it could be harder to keep the decisions so well in place, and I am worried about the U.S. segment of the business.

Conclusion

Nu Holdings continues to surprise me with its explosive growth and stock performance despite not being a U.S.-based company. With a 27% CAGR in its user base and an activity rate that only gets stronger, it remains one of the highest-quality growth plays in the financial sector. The fact that it trades at a lower P/E than SoFi while maintaining superior margins makes the current valuation, though high, feel justified.

The upcoming earnings call on February 25th should be interesting. While I expect strong results and maybe even higher acceleration in segments like landing, the "U.S. Bank" announcement is the new wild card. For now, I am maintaining my position, choosing to trust the management’s history of disciplined execution over my own fears of U.S. market saturation. Nu has surprised me before, and hopefully, it can continue doing so. I view NU stock still as a compelling buying opportunity.

data.title: Celsius: Stronger Than Before, But Is The Upside Still There?
data.description:
I like the energy drink category. In many ways, personally, I like to drink Monster (MNST) occasionally, and I liked it when I tried Celsius Holdings, Inc. (CELH) in the US, although it is not yet available in Brazil. But the other reason I like it is that it seems to me that it is still a category that should be able to grow significantly in the coming years, not only with the core organic energy drink, but also with related products and other optionalities that brands can invest in.

Since my last article on Celsius stock, there have been some positive changes in the case, and the market has recognized this less uncertain future and caused the stock to rise ~50% in about a year.

I still think Celsius is a good company, but the profile here has changed a bit. With this multiple expansion, we are no longer dealing with a high-risk, high-reward stock. The execution risk exists, and the prospects are encouraging, but now the margin of safety has decreased, and to unlock upside, the company will really need to prove that it can compound in the coming years.

Celsius Case Got Better

In my last article (check it here), Celsius was still having some optimization issues, and this has now been practically resolved, which alleviates some of the market's concerns. But another very important point is that, in the meantime, PepsiCo (PEP) has increased its stake in CELH.

This point is very important because, while there was some noise about this partnership before (due to lower purchases that occurred because of inventory issues), in recent quarters it has become clear that Celsius is central to PepsiCo's beverage strategy.

If Celsius lost PepsiCo's power, it could be a big problem for the case's prospects. In this competitive landscape, already dominated by Monster and Red Bull, the brand does matter, which is one of the positives for Celsius, but distribution and pricing matter even more in my view. By meeting these 3 “requirements”—a brand approved by the public, strong distribution power, and getting on the shelf with adequate pricing—Celsius has everything it needs to maintain a high market share and continue to grow.

But back to distribution, now that PEP has a more significant stake in Celsius, there have already been some interesting changes. Rockstar Energy is now managed by Celsius, and Alani Nu, a company recently acquired by Celsius, has also entered PepsiCo's distribution system, definitely a game changer.

For this reason, CELH's results have been very interesting recently. Revenue reached $725 million in Q3, an exceptional growth due to acquisitions, but Celsius also grew double-digit, not to mention that Alani Nu has super interesting momentum, growing +100% YoY. What impresses me the most is that the gross margin even increased in this period of high growth, although net income fell significantly due to some non-recurring items.

Now We Can Be More Optimistic About Celsius

Celsius still faces some uncertainties, mainly the highly competitive sector and a partnership with PepsiCo that, although now more solid, needs to continue proving that it is beneficial to both.

But probably, the execution risk is the most important here. For instance, even if PepsiCo continues to provide strong support and Monster/Red Bull do not make any aggressive moves, Celsius will still need to show that it knows how to capture optionalities, work with new brands, and continue to scale new products, as is the case with Alani Nu.

I mean, cases like Alani Nu attract attention for their explosive growth, but at the same time leave uncertainty as to how long this trend is sustainable, whether the brand will really be able to retain a target audience, or if it is just a passing fad.

But anyway, my main point is that everything I mentioned in the previous section enhances Celsius' prospects. Now we can be more confident in the execution, that compounding will occur not only through organic growth in the core but also with possible new movements such as international expansion.

Although Celsius is already big in the US, it still has huge addressable markets to capture. In Brazil, for example, there is no distribution (at least not in any significant way) of Celsius products. And according to Mordor, the total addressable market (TAM) for energy drinks in Brazil could reach ~$3.2 billion by 2031. If Celsius captures 10% of that, we are already talking about a $300 million increase in revenue. You may think this is not much, because Celsius' revenue is already almost ~$3 billion, but we are talking about just one country, one region of the world, among many others that Celsius could eventually capture.

Celsius Valuation: A Compounder… But The Margin Of Safety Isn't That Wide

We are talking about a company that already has more than $11 billion in market cap, so even if its revenue exceeds $3 billion and grows at a rapid pace, we also need the margin to advance gradually for all this valuation to make sense and for compounding to be achieved.

In the table below, I assumed that the top line will grow more rapidly in 2026 and 2027 (similar to what the market expects) due to the momentum of Alani Nu and Celsius' organic growth, but that even after that, the company will be able to maintain healthy and high revenue growth (~10% per year). In 2031, it would be able to reach almost $6 billion in revenue. This is a feasible assumption, as $6 billion is still less than what Monster delivered in 2024. In other words, we are not assuming that Celsius will dominate the entire energy drink market, only that it will perform well, maintain a high market share in the US, capture some optionalities, and expand internationally.

As for the margin, I have projected a gradual increase, reaching 13% in 2031, which is quite feasible considering that the adjusted EBITDA margin was already almost 30% in the last quarter. At this rate, compounding occurs, and in 2031, the P/E would already fall to less than 15x.

This should already allow for a very interesting upside in the long term. If in 2031 CELH stock trades at 25x fwd P/E (a “fair multiple,” but lower than MNST P/E, which is at ~40x now), the upside would be 73% in that period, or about 11% per year.

It's not a bad total return, especially since after 2031, Celsius may continue to deliver high compounding. But what bothers me is that the margin of safety is not that large. As there were many perceptible problems at Celsius before, as long as the company delivered reasonable growth, it was enough to unlock the upside.

Now, it needs to deliver as a healthy company, and delivering those numbers is not easy. As I mentioned, in this industry, you need brand, distribution, and pricing, and there is still a lot of competition, not only from players like Monster but also local competition.

So, even though I'm still optimistic about Celsius, I'm more cautious.

Bottom Line

I still think Celsius is a very interesting company, and with its partnership with PepsiCo stronger than ever, it is easier to envision a positive future. The valuation still allows for a good enough upside, but at the same time, I am more cautious because the company will have to continue to prove that it knows how to execute.

Next week, Celsius reports its Q4 earnings, and it seems to me to be a crucial moment for the company. Not only for it to report another solid quarter, in line with what it has already been delivering, but also to keep expectations for 2026 high. One of the points I want to hear about is how PepsiCo's distribution has impacted Alani Nu, and expectations are high, which ends up being a problem, since Celsius needs to show that the impact is very positive and that this is really a moat capable of igniting Celsius' growth, eventually leading to international expansion and new brands/products.

Therefore, I maintain a buy rating for CELH stock, but with a slightly more cautious outlook.

data.title: AWF: Fairly Valued Ahead Of Rate Cuts
data.description:
AllianceBernstein Global High Income Fund (AWF) is a steady fixed-income fund. While not the highest yield, its strategy has proven effective in protecting principal across cycles and macro events.

Concept of the Fund

AWF is an actively managed, closed-end fund that invests in a very wide variety of fixed-income securities, in corporate and government bonds around the world.

In the table above, the portfolio consisted of 1,249 holdings, reflecting just how much they diversify with this approach.

Almost two-thirds of the portfolio is concentrated on issuers from the United States, with the remainder being spread variously in other countries.

Just as the US is the preferred geography, the preferred type of security is corporate issues, which make up 90% of the portfolio when counting all credit ratings. We do see that non-investment grade is still 75% on its own.

A look at the portfolio by quality reveals that the largest concentration is the BB rating. This may help to explain their high portfolio turnover; "junk" bonds that are upgraded into investment grade can receive capital gains and allow them to roll gains into newer issues.

The most recent balance sheet from their semi-annual report shows us that AWF differs from many CEFs in that it doesn't really use its structure to finance its acquisitions with leverage to enhance yield and returns. Nearly all the portfolio is held through the equity capital of AWF shareholders, making it less risky in light of its preference for non-investment-grade bonds.

Overall, it's a fund that aims to be very opportunistic and diversified with its active approach.

Historical Results and Performance

As income is the primary goal of the fund, how has it fared in this mission over time?

With regular dividends paid monthly, we see that the fund has delivered on payment every year, with total distributions ranging between $0.75 and $1.00, the variance explained by special dividends for years that had additional income or capital gains that needed to be passed through to shareholders.

Despite this relative consistency, the price of the fund has been rather volatile, with dips pulling the more normal 7% yield as high as 10%. The main catalysts for these were 2020's COVID crash and then the Fed rate hikes that began in 2022. Rate hikes in particular are known to depress market values on fixed-income assets.

Where many bond funds, even some with "junk" securities, have price stability, the market has called AWF's holdings into doubt fairly often. As this comes to price per share, the past ten years saw a high of $13 and a low of about $9.

Nevertheless, the history of total returns reveals that AWF is a long-term compounder. While it's not an equity index, the fund has never crashed hard enough or faced delinquent issuers that caused investors reinvesting over the past decade to lose their principal.

Outlook and Valuation

As the price currently gives a yield of 7.85%, the main question is whether we think that will sustain and, assuming so, if we like what we get at that point.

What I will not do is focus too much on NAV per share in this case. Part of the reason is that steep discounts rarely occur. 2022's low of $9 per share was discounted to a NAV of $10.14 at the time. The lack of a distressed dividend shows that NAV tends to fluctuate based on the market value of its holdings. Subsequent recovery of NAV shows that favorable rotation or recovery of principal at maturity occurs. Moreover, it shows that coupons were not seriously affected.

Considering the diversification and lack of leverage that help contain the risks associated with these low-credit securities, I think the manager has executed well in capital preservation and is likely to do so going forward. I don't think NAV discounts are the idea here; pricing in a good yield is.

AWF is a fund that needs to be "bought well," to borrow a phrase from Howard Marks. With the occasional shift into double-digit yields, I think those are the best entry points for a fund like this. Otherwise, AWF seems like a relatively decent fund to hold and let dividends run off for reinvestment into more favorable portions of an income portfolio.

Another part of why I insist on this is the expense ratio, which is just over 1%. As this is a fixed-income fund, that's kind of high, and it creates friction that wears down returns over time. The better one buys, the more this impact can be priced in accordingly.

It is possible that rate cuts will raise the NAV and create capital gains in the near term. This seems likely as a new Fed Chairman will be appointed in May, likely to support President Trump's agenda for cuts.

Conclusion

In essence, I think AWF is fairly valued and becomes a Buy at a 10% dividend yield. Based on the current dividend, the share price would need to be $8.30 to create that yield. Holders can still collect distributions and may enjoy an opportunity for capital gains if the Fed cuts rates. For those reasons, I view AWF as a reasonably safe Hold at current prices.

data.title: CGDV: How This ETF Beats The S&P 500
data.description:
I have always thought that active management was not able to deliver alpha compared to indexed management in developed and relatively efficient market segments such as large-cap equities.

And it is what I kept telling myself while reading the official documents of Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV).

But a quick comparison with the S&P 500 is enough to realize that there is alpha here. At this point, the real question becomes: can there continue to be alpha tomorrow as well? My opinion: yes, even if I have to admit, not for the reasons I expected.

But before getting into the heart of the matter …

What Does The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF Do?

It is an actively managed ETF, but not fully flexible, as it follows a fairly defined process.

At its core, reading Capital Group’s informational material, there is a focus on dividend-paying companies or those with dividend potential, with a stated objective of exceeding the average dividend yield of the U.S. market. In exchange, investors pay an expense ratio of 0.33%, alongside a negligible median bid/ask spread of 0.02% and an almost zero premium/discount of 0.02%. It seems like a good compromise …

The Strategy

The ETF maintains a tilt toward large caps since it selects companies with market caps over $4B, maintaining a concentration in U.S.-centric companies. In these years of activity, it has always been fully invested, without showing any meaningful tactical liquidity management (it should not be confused with a “flexible” fund). To provide a clear summary for the reader, in my opinion, it can be outlined as follows:

  • ≥ 80% in dividend-paying stocks.
  • ≥ 90% in equity.
  • ≥ 90% in companies with at least an investment-grade rating.
  • Large-cap UA (over $4B market cap).
  • Up to 10% ex-US.

Active management, therefore, is found in the work of the Capital System multi-manager structure, where each portfolio manager manages an autonomous “sleeve” with a sort of multi-style bottom-up selection (in my way of interpreting the situation).

The Holding Distribution

Referring to the facts, actually, at least from a personal analysis, I did not initially find strong consistency in the holding distribution compared to the assumptions I read in the prospectus.

This holding distribution does not seem too aligned with the concept of “Dividend Value,” although overall the management has remained well anchored to the fixed range set out in the prospectus.

However, here we see about 52 holdings where the Top 10 account for about 42% of the portfolio, which does not at all resemble a classic value ETF. Rather, in my view, it can be reinterpreted as value reinterpreted through a profitability and tech-leadership lens, considering an exposure of 25% to tech, which includes stocks like NVIDIA that weigh up to 5.9%.

The Results

The fact is that this management has worked particularly well in recent years, which is why the “critical” side has remained silent, preferring instead to give weight to the results. In several respects, I must admit that I was pleasantly surprised as well:

It Has Generated Alpha Over A Multi-Year Cycle

And there are a series of very distinctive data points in this regard:

  • The most interesting is R-squared, here equal to 77 versus the S&P 500: a figure that I, personally, interpret as meaning that there is 23% of behavior “not explained” by the S&P. An element was confirmed by the Beta at 0.85. Well … an ETF that replicates the market would have an R² close to 95+ and a beta close to 1. Therefore, the growth drivers of CGDV’s returns do not exactly match those of the S&P 500.
  • This ex post turned out to be a good thing because it generated a capture ratio (Downside/Upside) equal to 83/103. I’ll read it differently by telling you that the fund lost less than the S&P 500 (it captured only 83% of the drawdowns) but gained more during rallies (103%).
  • While taking on competitive risk, dictated by a standard deviation of 11.32.

So in fact, CGDV did not just beat the S&P. It did so:

  • With lower Beta.
  • With lower downside.
  • With higher Sharpe.
  • With moderate R² (real activation).

It Has Maintained Competitive Dividends (Although Not Too Much)

We said that the dividend target is 30% above the S&P yield. In this sense, it is fairly in line with its objective, although, let’s be honest, this is not where CGDV’s alpha comes from, even though, from a “communication” standpoint, the management company has, in my opinion, pushed the accelerator quite hard in this direction.

A Comparison You Need To Know

The question I ask myself is: is this alpha structural or dependent on the mega-cap dominance regime? In answering, I take as reference a recent coverage of mine, JIVE, focusing on the 6-month performance.

JIVE represents a truly active global value strategy, and I think it is extremely interesting to note how, in the annual sector rotation process we are witnessing, CGDV has not been able to outperform its performance.

I would say that, as far as I am concerned, this actually sends a clear message about the “real” direction and purpose of the fund, not the one from the prospectus: to maintain an “intelligent” allocation relative to the S&P 500. Therefore, an index that today is dominated by growth solutions.

Risk

That being said, I believe CGDV’s outperformance should clearly be read as a positive ability by the management team to interpret the market opportunistically within a mega-cap dominance context. Not, for example, within a sector rotation environment, as we are experiencing today.

The fund’s flexibility does not appear to extend in that direction. Clearly, the selection remains anchored to certain “constraints” that, in my opinion, make the fund fairly tied to the U.S. mega-cap regime. This is why it is underperforming today compared to solutions like JIVE, which instead move precisely within the broader global value macro category.

My Opinion

To streamline the logical process described above, I will tell you that personally I consider it a valid alternative to funds like SPY, but not a 100% substitute for the core position of the portfolio.

Today, those who follow me know that I, personally, continue to appreciate an allocation close to the principles of Ray Dalio’s all weather portfolio.

Conclusion

Finally, I think the right rating for CGDV is BUY because compared to the S&P 500, it has managed to deliver concrete results. In this sector rotation process, it continues to show strength relative to the S&P 500, but it suffers when compared to truly value active management solutions. I therefore do not see it as a total substitute for the core component of the portfolio, although I appreciate the value it brings in terms of contribution to returns relative to the S&P 500

data.title: Wingstop: Can The Moat Survive The Fried Chicken War? (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Taking a page from what I wrote a year ago, Wingstop Inc. (WING) was flying a little too close to the sun. And, like Icarus, it came back down.

But let's not exaggerate. The drop was roughly 20%, and the stock is now sitting almost exactly within the tight short- to medium-term range I outlined back then.

Since then, a lot has happened.

If you ask me, the biggest change was the pullback in the multiples of many fast-casuals like Chipotle (CMG), Shake Shack (SHAK), and Sweetgreen (SG).

As the macro cooled off, many of the 2024/2025 market darlings had to be repriced, with investors pricing in slower traffic.

Specifically, Wingstop found itself in a sort of "Fried Chicken War."

From new chains like Dave’s Hot Chicken to long-standing ones, everyone seems to be adding more sauces and add-ons to satisfy Gen Z's preference for hyper-customization (I even told you this would become a trend in my Ultimate Guide to Restaurants in 2026).

I think the sign of the times was when KFC (YUM) opened a satellite concept called "Saucy" to run sauce combinations and observe how this affects the average check per guest.

After all, sauces are cheap to make and, consequently, have very high margins and strong appeal to both small and large orders.

Same-store sales fell in three of the four quarters of FY 2025 (probably driven by traffic), and Wingstop is already trading at multiples well below the 5-year average.

And this is where you really need to pay attention.

This is an asset-light chain, and it should be analyzed as such.

Mature asset-light chains like Yum! Brands and Restaurant Brands (QSR) naturally tend to trade at much higher multiples than restaurants operating their own portfolio for the simple reason that they are not at the mercy of tight restaurant-level margins.

But that doesn’t mean you blindly accept sky-high multiples for a concept that’s slowing down.

That’s really the bind Wingstop finds itself in today.

Fried Chicken War Leaving Its Mark

You don't have to dig very deep to notice that Wingstop's same-store sales have plummeted recently.

It rose slightly in Q1, with same-store sales up 0.5%, but fell 1.9% in Q2 and plunged 5.6% in Q3 and a further 5.8% in Q4. This makes sense when you note that things deteriorated sequentially in H2 and bottomed out in December.

And it's not just them.

If you stack them with other fast-casual peers, you'll notice a broader consumer pullback, mainly due to a more value-driven environment starting with H2 2025 (casual dining running value offers and, more recently, a second wave of $5 deals in fast food).

In addition to that, other chains within the QSR space are doubling down on fried chicken and add-ons, as I told you before.

And since I like to create terms (examples include "Fast-Food Exodus" and "Taco Bell-ification"), I'm going to call this movement the "Fried Chicken War."

I see—and I believe most of you see it too—Wingstop positioning itself as a sort of "flavor expert" (12 flavors in order of spiciness) and dipping sauces. The strategy here is bundles such as the 'Boneless Meal Deal,' built for small groups (the CEO said the core is dinner for two), not bargain-bin $5 promotions.

We have a clear view into our demand space, our core consumer and the opportunity in front of us. Our core demand space is an off-premise occasion, typically involving two or more adults eating together where a high-quality indulgent experience matters most. Flavors, variety and cook to order are top of mind for our targeted consumer and are core to what Wingstop has delivered for decades, but we also know these consumers expect a fast and consistent experience.

The price of boneless wings at chains like Popeyes usually ranges from ~$1.00 to $1.20 per piece depending on the portion and flavor. This can be 20 to 30% more expensive at Wingstop precisely because you are paying for the hyper-customization.

The main issue here is that Wingstop’s customization advantage is no longer exclusive, as lower-priced chains now offer similar sauce varieties at cheaper price points.

And of course, you wouldn't need to order many pieces to have three or more flavors. Just pay for more sauces.

Saucy is simply a sign of the times.

On the other side of the "Fried Chicken War," we have chains like Chick-fil-A that continue to gain market share even with low-income customers visiting restaurants less frequently.

This is interesting because Chick-fil-A has a large footprint in the Southern United States (both have a very strong overlap in Texas, which is the largest market for both chains).

I suspect there's some trade-down happening, especially at lunchtime, with guests choosing cheaper chicken sandwiches instead of higher-ticket items.

One very interesting point here is that Raising Cane's is probably one of the fastest-growing chains and has a playbook of extreme menu simplification (including the sauces, with Cane's Sauce being the centerpiece).

So, how does Wingstop plan to bring back low-income customers? After all, this is essential for them to reach their $3 million AUV target (that would be a $1 million bump per location, considering the AUV is at $2 million).

From what I've heard, they'll run the "Wingstop Is Here" campaign to increase brand reach while working on their new loyalty program called Club Wingstop.

No value menus, no aggressive promotions.

Even Shake Shack, with its New York-heavy footprint, ended up rolling out a prix fixe meal. That tells you something. In my view, Wingstop may need a similar entry-level offer to win back lower-income guests.

The FY 2026 guidance already incorporates this. Management expects same-store sales within the United States to remain flat, even with easier comparisons this time.

This tells me that:

  1. The guest is paying less for hyper-customization, which makes one of their biggest competitive advantages commoditized;
  2. AUV will likely remain frozen until they recover traffic, as fast-casual chains are having trouble with the elasticity of the average check per guest while fast-food chains are attacking with value promotions;
  3. Unless they act strategically, same-store sales will only start growing again when a combination of menu innovations and a stronger macro environment come together;
  4. Specific occasions like bundling on special days (as happened during the Super Bowl) remain strong, but this does not sustain traffic in the long term.

But even after losing the edge of premiumizing its wings, Wingstop still has another advantage up its sleeve.

If you didn't already know, they are in the top tier of restaurant-level economies in the industry. And that's precisely why they manage to maintain approximately 10 to 15% annual unit growth even with same-store sales plummeting.

Keep An Eye On The Real Edge

I think I've already broken down the numbers for you, but I think we should take another look with the updated figures at how cost-effective Wingstop restaurants are.

To give you an idea, construction costs are approximately $580,000 (generally build-to-suit and focused on takeout/delivery), which is extremely low even for QSRs.

Potbelly's most cost-effective restaurants cost ~$650,000, while Sweetgreen's ($1.7 million with Infinite Kitchen) and Shake Shack's (lean model) cost $3.3 million. In other words, they are definitely in the top quartile when it comes to low construction costs.

With AUVs of $2 million and restaurant-level margins between 20% and 25%, franchisees are generating restaurant-level EBITDA of $400,000 to $500,000 annually. This means a payback period of approximately 2 or 3 years, considering franchise and marketing fees of 10% on gross sales.

Note that this is equivalent to the ROI of a QSR pizza chain like Domino's (DPZ), which is known for its cost-effectiveness and different store formats.

Of course, we are looking at the restaurant-level margins reported by the few company-owned Wingstop restaurants (they represent only 2% of the total portfolio and are mostly concentrated in urban areas in Texas), but franchised restaurants should not exceed 4 years for payback.

Management believes that if unit economics remain this attractive and AUVs increase (as I showed you, this part is not so easy), 10,000 restaurants worldwide becomes achievable.

If they grow their footprint at the target of 10% annually, they would reach this number in FY 2038 with $30 billion in systemwide sales and Wingstop something close to $3 billion or slightly more (considering company-owned locations) in revenue.

Something the CEO called a "game changer" for achieving this goal is the Smart Kitchen—a digital kitchen system powered by AI.

Currently, it's already running in all restaurants in the United States and is reducing order preparation time by approximately 40%, consequently generating greater customer satisfaction and reduced turnover.

I don't see it the same way as the CEO. Prototypical kitchen models have been called that before, especially in fast-casual restaurants (see Sweetgreen's Infinity Kitchen or Chipotle's Augmented Makeline).

Since the Smart Kitchen is cheaper than the two models I mentioned (both are robotic), it could increase margins by a low single digit in the medium term.

Lowering The Flight

You can clearly see that from now on, revenue growth comes entirely from the opening of new units and no longer from sales in the same stores.

That's why, starting in Q2 FY 2025, they slowed down considerably:

With the supply chain benefits from the wings that I covered in the last article, they are seeing small margin benefits that are putting them above the 5-year average. EBITDA, for example, grew just over 13% to $210.1 million for the entire FY 2025, outperforming revenue over the same period.

Cash flow data is not yet available, but since the EBITDA-to-OCF conversion rate is between 80 and 90%, we should probably expect an OCF of nearly $180 million and an FCF of $150 million (considering slightly lower Capex compared to last year due to the Smart Kitchen installation).

Some Red Flags Of Capital Allocation

As you can see, even though Wingstop's sales are slowing down, FCF is growing a little faster due to lower Capex. And, to avoid stagnation, they are returning capital to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks.

It's a little strange to see a growth company do this, but let's take a closer look to understand it.

To begin with, they pay an annual dividend of $1.20 per share (just a 0.43% dividend yield) that grows at a double-digit rate annually. For FY 2026, Wall St. is targeting dividends of $1.31 per share, growing just over 9% year-over-year.

Regarding share buybacks, they have already repurchased and retired 2.5 million shares since they began doing so more frequently in FY 2023. But from FY 2024 to FY 2025, they repurchased approximately 1.2 million net shares, reducing the share count to 27.5 million (buyback yield of 4.1%).

Since they have $91.3 million authorized for use, and assuming they exhaust it in FY 2026, at an average repurchase price of $260 per share, they would repurchase just over 351,000 (a buyback yield of just under 1.5%).

Calculating how much they would need to spend on repurchases and dividends to return a shareholder yield of a mere 2%, this would amount to $126.8 million.

That's almost all the FCF they would generate in the entire year!

Now, if the stores have such a high ROI, why don't they use this FCF to open their own stores (perhaps like Potbelly did, setting up a clustering strategy in high-demand areas or even moving to lower DMA areas in the North/Northwest), creating more value for shareholders?

This might tell us that perhaps high-density markets like Houston, Chicago, Los Angeles, etc., are saturated or at risk of cannibalizing franchises. Or perhaps it's a way to maintain the narrative of EPS growth from share buybacks that "artificially" (note the quotation marks) beat Wall Street estimates.

In any case, it doesn't seem like the best decision to buy back shares at these levels and only shows that they lack optionality.

I think that if they decided to move towards a portfolio that's perhaps 80/20, still leaning towards franchised stores, they would be creating much more value for shareholders than the low single digits they are currently delivering.

Low-DMA expansion may stretch the payback period, yet it strengthens brand presence and makes them somewhat less vulnerable to competition.

Too Stretched?

If you recall, I once had a price target in the $200s for Wingstop. A lot has changed since then.

The big shift was the slowdown, which brought EPS CAGR expectations down with it.

To give you an idea, EPS for FY 2026 was expected to be around $5.74 two years ago, growing at a CAGR above 20% until FY 2031 to $15.70. Currently, Wall Street expects EPS of $4.69 in FY 2026, a sharp slowdown compared to the normalized EPS of $5.59 in FY 2025.

I confess that it's very difficult to find ways to evaluate a growth stock like Wingstop, but I believe the best way is by reverse engineering some models to assess if this is truly credible.

For example, if we assume they actually reach an EPS of $20.33 in FY 2034 and consider that Wingstop trades at the same multiples as a mature, high-growth fast-casual like Chipotle (P/E of ~33x), the price target would be ~$195, already discounting this to present value from a cost of equity of 14.8% (Wingstop has a very high beta of 1.82).

Going a little further, I told you that revenue could reach $3 billion in FY 2038, and, based on the estimated revenue conversion rate in FY 2034 and its EPS, we could assume an EPS of ~$30 for Wingstop's maturity (10,000 locations worldwide). In this case, we would also find a price target below $200, discounting this to present value with the same P/E multiple.

Let's use a different metric here.

Considering Graham's Model and an EPS of $4.69 for FY 2026, we would arrive at a price target of ~$215 using a growth rate based on the CAGR from FY 2026 to FY 2034 from Wall Street estimates.

The current price of $260 would only be justifiable in this model if we aimed for a growth rate of 25%, and that's without even considering the safety margin that Ben Graham used (usually 25%).

The CAGR of EPS from FY 2016 to FY 2025 was almost 33%, which tells us that if they pull exactly the same growth from now on, the stock would be at its fair price considering the 25% safety margin.

It turns out that now they are dealing with:

  1. Weak same-store sales;
  2. Fierce competition and commoditization of hyper-customization; saturation of urban markets;
  3. Loss of the novelty effect from the beginning of the expansion.

They probably won't be able to achieve a similar CAGR going forward.

I would say Wingstop (based on a scenario of flat comps, very strong competition, and continued value destruction by buying back shares at very high multiples) is a Sell trading at a P/E of 50x.

I think things get more interesting if the stock slips into the $200s with a P/E below 50x and pricing in a CAGR of ~20% in EPS.

If they change their strategy to something more aggressive (value promotions, more competitive bundles, etc.) or lock in buybacks and dividends to bet on build-to-suit stores in areas with low DMAs, I might change this rating.

It’s still a high-quality business and a solid cash machine, but the valuation feels stretched.

data.title: Rheinmetall: Germany's Defense Spending Surge Should Be A Windfall For The Stock
data.description:
I’ll be the first to admit to my own mistakes. I called Rheinmetall AG (RNMBY) a buy in late September last year only for the price to plummet soon after. But the stock has since recovered a bit and looks to be on the way back up.

In the long term I remain bullish on it due to a couple of factors, namely German rearmament and Rheinmetall’s contracts with other European militaries and the United States military. However, I have revised my price target in light of where the stock is trading now and its projected earnings.

So, read on for my updated investment thesis on Rheinmetall.

The Opportunity: Germany's Arsenal For Land And Sea

Rheinmetall’s chief competitive advantage is that it’s on the absolute bleeding edge of military hardware, which has caused its vehicles and artillery to be adopted by many countries aside from its native Germany.

The company is one of Germany’s oldest arms producers. It began in 1889 as an artillery manufacturer in Dusseldorf. Today it produces far more than artillery, though that remains a core part of its business.

The company also produces vehicles ranging from logistics trucks to tanks and has expanded into ship production following its acquisition of Naval Vessels Lürssen in 2025, which it plans to close in early 2026.

One of Rheinmetall’s most famous products is the Leopard 2 tank, which it produces along with a few other smaller German defense contractors. The Leopard 2 is one of the most widely used tanks in the world, with seventeen militaries fielding it, including Germany, Spain, Poland, Canada, Turkey, and Ukraine.

It’s also involved in producing Britain’s Challenger 3 tank, namely its 120mm L55A1 cannon, and supplies its Lynx IFV to a number of militaries including the United States which awarded a contract to Rheinmetall for the Lynx to the tune of $700 million. The Lynx is also being sent to Ukraine as of mid-January 2026. Other adopters of the Lynx include Romania, Greece, Brazil, Italy, Hungary, and Indonesia.

The last vehicle I want to talk about is Rheinmetall’s Panther Kf51, a next-generation main battle tank that includes some interesting hardware likely inspired by lessons learned in Ukraine, where the Leopard 2 has encountered some serious issues.

The Leopard 2 performs best when it’s fielded by a military with air superiority, which Ukraine does not have in this war. Other problems include an overcomplicated design that makes repairs difficult and a vulnerability to drones, which is shared by the other tanks of its generation, like America’s M1 Abrams and Russia’s T-90.

I can’t speak to how difficult the Panther will be to repair, but it is equipped with some interesting air support and anti-drone tools like an automated machine gun mounted on the back of the turret to shoot down enemy drones and 4 HERO120 loitering munitions so the Panther can bring its own air support if the enemy has control of the sky.

It isn’t especially focused on air warfare, but it does produce components and systems for jets like the F-35 along with drones and loitering munitions. And with its 60% joint venture with ICEYE, Rheinmetall is also branching out into satellite and space warfare.

Now, on to the growth potential for Rheinmetall and why I’m very bullish on it in the long term.

The Catalyst: Germany's Rapid Rearmament

In my first article on Rheinmetall, I talked a lot about the fact that global defense spending is on the rise, particularly in Europe. But I am revising my thesis slightly. The rest of Europe increasing their defense spending will help Rheinmetall, but it’s the company’s domestic market in Germany that ought to drive the bulk of its growth.

And Rheinmetall is the leading defense contractor of its kind in Germany. There are other defense contractors to be sure, like Airbus and MTU Aero Engines, but they are far narrower in scope and mainly focused on aerospace, not land or sea warfare.

Germany is rearming incredibly quickly. As Europe’s largest economy and the world’s third largest behind the U.S. and China, Germany historically often ranked in the top ten countries for military expenditure.

But Germany has, in the past couple of years, more than doubled its 2020 military budget from $52 billion then to $136.9 billion by the end of 2026, catapulting its spending ahead of India, Saudi Arabia, and the United Kingdom to become the world’s fourth-largest military spender behind the U.S., China, and Russia.

That budget is set to continue growing to meet NATO’s 3.5% GDP spending on the military by 2029 and transform the Bundeswehr into Europe’s largest military force. Rheinmetall, as one of Germany’s premier military contractors expects to absorb a good chunk of that money.

Largely due to that rearmament budget, Rheinmetall is anticipating a 30% CAGR in sales for the whole of 2025 over 2024 and a 45% CAGR in its backlog. Neither figure is too far a reach when you consider the company’s latest results (Q3 2025, reported November 2026).

The Money: Expanding Influence, Expanding Profits

In Q3 2025, Rheinmetall reported sales of €2.7 billion, up 13% over Q3 2024, and it grew its backlog 23% to €63.8 billion. It did grow its capex 1.5 points to 8.9%, but its operating margin also grew 0.7 points to 12.9%.

And most of that capex came from Rheinmetall expanding its production capabilities to meet the huge amounts of orders coming in from Germany and other European militaries.

And Q3 wasn’t an aberration; it was the continuation of a long-term growth trend for Rheinmetall. Over the past 3 years, its revenue has grown at a CAGR of 23%. Compared to American defense contractors like General Dynamics (GD), that’s explosive growth.

Actually, General Dynamics makes for a good 1:1 comparison; it’s about the same size as Rheinmetall in terms of market cap. Rheinmetall's 3-year revenue CAGR is more than double General Dynamics’ 10%.

I don’t expect that growth streak to come to an end anytime soon. Rheinmetall was expecting record nominations for Q4 2025, the quarter Germany passed its 2026 budget (November 28, 2025).

On the technical front, Rheinmetall’s RSI is sitting at 53 right now, exactly in the range I like to see, so it should have plenty of runway ahead of it.

There are a couple of worrying indicators with Rheinmetall though. First, it has a high debt load of $2.85 billion relative to its cash of $653.9 million. I don’t like to see debt that high relative to cash. Second, its valuation is concerning; it was when I first covered the stock as well. Rheinmetall’s TTM P/E ratio is 86.36 compared to a sector median of 21.62.

However, I think that’s made up for due to Rheinmetall’s absurd growth relative to its sector. Year-over-year, its revenue is growing 24.61%, which is 450.82% higher than the sector median of 4.47%. EPS growth too is incredible, with its year-over-year diluted EPS growth coming in at 23.5% compared to the sector median of 7.81%. Operating cash flow is growing 78.75% year over year relative to the sector median of 9.31%.

Rheinmetall also has better margins than others in its sector, with a gross margin of 52.13% compared to a sector median of 31.39% and a net income margin of 7.64% compared to the sector median of 6.72%.

That’s why I’m still comfortable calling Rheinmetall a buy, particularly with the German government’s spending plans solidified in Berlin’s latest budget.

My updated price projection for Rheinmetall is based on the consensus EPS estimate of €40.07 for the end of 2026. I’m using the European stock, which trades under the symbol RHM on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange, for the sake of easy math, but the ADR with the symbol RNMBY will follow the gains or losses of its European counterpart. However, it’s a 1:5 ADR, so five shares of RNMBY are equal to one share of RHM.

Anyway, take the EPS estimate of 40.07 and multiply it by Rheinmetall’s projected P/E ratio of 45.41 by the end of 2026, and we get a price target of €1,819 for the European stock, which is a 4.3% increase from current levels.

However, if we take the EPS estimate and multiply it by Rheinmetall’s current forward P/E ratio of 65.82, we get €2,637, which is a 51% increase over current prices.

In light of the dip Rheinmetall took after I first covered it, that seems a bit optimistic to me. So I think the best estimate is between the two, which puts us at about €2,200, or 26% above current levels. The ADR is trading at about $409, so my price target for it is about $515 by the end of 2026.

The Risks: I Hope I'm Wrong

Aside from the debt and valuation risks I mentioned above, the biggest risk to Rheinmetall and indeed all other defense contractors is a reduction in tension around the world.

An end to the Russia-Ukraine War, for instance, would be fantastic for the world but would hurt Rheinmetall’s bottom line, as it would take pressure off of Germany to rearm as quickly or to the extent that it plans to.

Unfortunately, despite some hope I had earlier in the year that it would end soon, as of the latest news, there is no end in sight for that conflict at the moment. Which means Germany’s rearmament plans are likely going to continue apace.

I hope I'm wrong, but I fear I'm right, and with global tensions on the rise outside of Europe, I don’t think Germany will be the only country bolstering its military over the next several years.

The Verdict: Still A Buy Despite The Dip

Even with the dip in late 2025, Rheinmetall still looks like the best way to play German and broader European rearmament in response to the first major European war since World War II.

Rheinmetall has a competitive advantage due to its advanced technology, and its equipment has been extensively tested in Ukraine, which means it has recent experience with the threats of the modern battlefield, particularly drones, which have been one of the most widely used weapons of the Russia-Ukraine war.

The company’s valuation might be high, but so is its growth, and it’s uniquely positioned to absorb a solid chunk of the continent’s largest economy, building the continent’s largest military.

Give Rheinmetall a look for a long-term buy and hold.

data.title: Meta Platforms: Recent Significant Decline And The Longer-Term Story
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Pershing Square Holdings H2 2025 Letter To Shareholders.

At the end of last year, we initiated a significant position in Meta (META), a company we have followed closely for many years. Alongside Alphabet (GOOGL), another one of our core holdings, Meta is the dominant leader in the highly attractive and secularly fast-growing digital advertising space. The company principally owns and operates a “Family of Apps” (Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp) with an entrenched global user base of over 3.5 billion daily active users. This vast, diverse, and highly engaged audience is the source of a powerful network effect: as more users engage more deeply with Meta’s apps, the apps become incrementally more valuable to both users and advertisers, creating an exceptionally durable competitive advantage. The company’s deep insights into consumer behavior and interests allow advertisers to precisely target specific demographics and intentions, making Meta an indispensable and essential marketing platform for businesses seeking highly effective ad spend.

Our opportunity to invest in Meta arose following the company’s Q3 2025 earnings call in October when it announced it would spend significantly more on AI initiatives in 2026 than investors had expected, leading to a 20% share price decline. As a result, we were able to establish our position in Meta at a very attractive valuation of just 20 times earnings per share, earnings which include the significant losses from its Reality Labs segment that amount to nearly 25% of the company’s overall profit. Excluding the Reality Labs losses, the implied market valuation for Meta’s core advertising business was less than 16 times earnings per share. This valuation represented a significant discount to the S&P 500 for one of the world’s great businesses that is currently growing its revenues in excess of 20% per annum.

In our view, concerns surrounding Meta's elevated spending overlook the transformative potential of AI to accelerate near-term revenue growth and reinforce its long-term competitive moats. We believe Meta is one of the clearest beneficiaries of AI and is already seeing material impact across its core business drivers: content recommendation, user engagement, ad monetization and new product innovation.

Improved AI content recommendation systems are driving material growth in user engagement, especially for the video format with time spent watching videos on Instagram up more than 30% year-over-year. Moreover, as content on Meta's platforms has increasingly broadened from the "social graph" (friends/family/followed accounts) to include a wider range of interest-based content from a diverse group of creators, AI recommendation engines enable the company to better understand, surface, and curate content that users desire. Likewise, AI greatly improves ad monetization by leveraging Meta's rich first-party data to deliver more relevant and targeted ads. Automation capabilities – from the company's Advantage+ suite to its generative AI ad creation tools – facilitate an end-to-end self-serve model for advertisers. We are also encouraged by AI's potential to unlock new commercial use cases and engagement modes. For instance, the rollout of Business AI on Meta's messaging platforms could be a game-changer for businesses to pursue scalable, low-cost customer engagement and wearables like Meta Ray-Bans, which present new avenues to leverage AI interactively in the real world.

We believe the substantial upside potential from AI, both within Meta's compute-constrained core business and in its new initiatives, supports front-loading investments in infrastructure and talent. After Meta's next year's planned spending ramp, we expect the company to resume its rapid long-term earnings growth algorithm. Notably, in its year-end earnings call at the end of January, management stated that the near-term acceleration in revenue growth would allow it to continue to grow operating income in 2026 despite significantly elevated levels of investment spending. In addition, the company noted that losses at the Reality Labs division will peak in 2026 and moderate in future years. Should Meta's high levels of revenue growth slow down or the returns on its AI investments be lower than anticipated, we are confident the company will be able to retain the operational levers – reminiscent of the 2023 "Year of Efficiency" – to moderate expenses, maintain profit margins, and deliver continued strong earnings-per-share growth.

Our investment in Meta highlights our approach of closely following a watchlist of high-quality businesses and waiting patiently for an attractive entry price. Having recently trimmed and sold other holdings, we held sufficient cash to move quickly and invest in a business we have long admired that has experienced a significant decline in its share price in recent months due to near-term concerns, which we do not believe will impact the longer-term Meta story.

Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. All investments involve risk, including the loss of principal. Please see accompanying endnotes and disclaimers.

DISCLAIMERS

Limitations of Performance Data

This report contains information and analyses relating to all publicly disclosed equity positions above 50 basis points in the Company's portfolio during 2025 and 2026.
